en.extract:
The imperial shag or imperial cormorant (Leucocarbo atriceps) is a black-and-white cormorant native to southern South America, islands of the Subantarctic, and the Antarctic Peninsula, primarily in rocky coastal regions, but locally also at large inland lakes. Some taxonomic authorities, including the International Ornithologists' Union, place it in the genus Leucocarbo, others in the genus Phalacrocorax. It is also known as the blue-eyed shag, blue-eyed cormorant and by many other names, and is one of a larger group of cormorants called blue-eyed shags. The taxonomy is complex, and several types are treated as either subspecies or separate species.


Taxonomy
The imperial shag was formally described in 1828 by the Royal Navy officer Phillip Parker King based on a specimen that he had collected in the Strait of Magellan of southern Chile. He placed the species in the genus Phalacrocorax and coined the binomial name Phalacrocorax atriceps, where the specific epithet combines the Latin ater meaning "black" with -ceps meaning "-capped" or "-headed". The imperial shag was formerly usually retained in the genus Phalacrocorax, but has also sometimes been placed in its own genus Notocarbo. Based on a comprehensive molecular phylogeny of the cormorant family Phalacrocoracidae by Martyn Kennedy and Hamish Spencer that was published in 2014, the imperial shag is now one of nine species placed in the genus Leucocarbo that had been introduced by the French naturalist Charles Lucien Bonaparte in 1856.
Eight subspecies are recognised:

L. a. atriceps (King, PP, 1828) – islands and coasts of south-central Chile and Argentina southward to Cape Horn
L. a. albiventer (Lesson, RP, 1831) – King shag – Falkland Islands
L. a. georgianus (Lönnberg, E, 1906) – South Georgia shag – South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands, and South Orkney Islands
L. a. melanogenis (Blyth, E, 1860) – Crozet shag – Prince Edward, Marion, and Crozet islands (southern Indian Ocean, southeast of South Africa)
L. a. bransfieldensis (Murphy, RC, 1936) – Antarctic shag – South Shetland Islands and Antarctic Peninsula
L. a. verrucosus (Cabanis, JL, 1875) – Kerguelen shag – Kerguelen Islands (southern Indian Ocean)
L. a. nivalis (Falla, RA, 1937) – Heard Island shag – Heard Island (southern Indian Ocean)
L. a. purpurascens (Brandt, JF, 1837) – Macquarie shag – Macquarie Island and adjacent Bishop and Clerk Rocks (southeast of Australia)

While some authorities consider all of the above — except albiventer — as separate species, others consider all as subspecies of a single species (as done in this article). Alternatively, some recognize two species, the white-cheeked L. atriceps (with subspecies bransfieldensis, nivalis and georgianus) and the black-cheeked L. albiventer (with subspecies melanogenis and purpurascens), or it has been suggested that three species should be recognized: L. atriceps (including albiventer), L. georgianus (with subspecies bransfieldensis and nivalis), and L. melanogenis (with subspecies purpurascens and possibly verrucosus, though the latter is more distinctive, and most consider it a separate species, the Kerguelen shag).
All these taxa are now treated as subspecies of the imperial shag based on a molecular phylogenetic study by Nicolas Rawlence and collaborators that was published in 2022. The authors found that despite the wide separation between the breeding colonies on the subantarctic islands, the genetic differences are small suggesting a circumpolar dispersal from South America after the Last Glacial Maximum around 21 thousand years ago.


Description

The imperial shag has a total length of 70–79 cm (28–31 in) and weighs 1.8–3.5 kg (4.0–7.7 lb), with males usually larger than females. It is endowed with glossy black feathers covering most of its body, with a white belly and neck. It possesses a distinctive ring of blue skin around its eyes, an orange-yellow nasal knob, pinkish legs and feet, and an erectile black crest. During the non-breeding season, adults lack the crest, have a duller facial area, and less or no white to the back and wings. It has a serrated bill used for catching fish.
The group varies primarily in the amount of white on the cheeks/ear-coverts, wing-coverts and back. Most taxa have white cheeks and ear-coverts, but these are black in albiventer, purpurascens and melanogenis. Chicks are uniform brownish, and immatures are brownish and white (instead of black and white), have dull facial skin, and lack the orange-yellow nasal knob and blue eye-ring.


Behavior


Breeding

This is a colonial, monogamous species. The colonies are usually relatively small, but some consist of hundreds of pairs and are often shared with other seabirds such as rock shags, southern rockhopper penguins and black-browed albatrosses. Up to five eggs (usually two or three) are placed in a nest made of seaweed and grass, and cemented together with mud and excrement. The eggs usually hatch in about five weeks, and are brooded by both parents. Many chicks and eggs are lost to predators such as skuas and sheathbills.


Feeding
The diet of this species consists of small benthic fish, crustaceans, polychaetes, gastropods and octopuses. They primarily feed on fish, especially Argentine anchoita,. Mean diving depth is almost 25 m (82 ft), and they have been filmed diving as deep as 60 m (200 ft) to forage on the sea floor. Most feeding takes place in inshore regions, but at least some populations will travel some distance from the shore to fish.


Conservation status
Overall this species is not considered threatened and is consequently listed as Least Concern by BirdLife International and IUCN. Most subspecies are relatively common, with estimates of over 10,000 pairs of each.


Gallery


Notes


References

The '''imperial shag''' or '''imperial cormorant''' ('''''Leucocarbo atriceps''''') is a black-and-white [[cormorant]] native to southern [[South America]], islands of the [[Subantarctic]], and the [[Antarctic Peninsula]], primarily in rocky coastal regions, but locally also at large inland lakes. Some taxonomic authorities, including the [[International Ornithologists' Union]], place it in the [[genus]] ''Leucocarbo'', others in the genus ''Phalacrocorax''. It is also known as the '''blue-eyed shag''', '''blue-eyed cormorant''' and by many other names, and is one of a larger group of cormorants called [[blue-eyed shag]]s.<ref name=Nelson>{{Citation | last = Nelson | first = J. Bryan | year = 2006 | title = Pelicans, Cormorants, and Their Relatives: The Pelecaniformes | publisher = Oxford University Press, U.S.A. | pages = 489–493, Plate 8 | isbn = 978-0-19-857727-0}}</ref> The [[Taxonomy (biology)|taxonomy]] is complex, and several types are treated as either [[subspecies]] or separate species.

==Taxonomy==
The imperial shag was [[species description|formally described]] in 1828 by the [[Royal Navy]] officer [[Phillip Parker King]] based on a specimen that he had collected in the [[Strait of Magellan]] of southern Chile. He placed the species in the genus ''[[Phalacrocorax]]'' and coined the [[binomial name]] ''Phalacrocorax atriceps'',<ref>{{ cite journal | last=King | first=Phillip Parker | author-link=Phillip Parker King | date=1828 | title=Extracts from a letter addressed by Capt. Phillip Parker King, R.N., F.R.S. and L.S., to N. A. Vigors, Esq., on the animals of the Straits of Magellan | journal=Zoological Journal | volume=4 | pages=91-105 [102] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/27485018 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Cottrell | editor2-first=G. William | year=1979 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=1 | edition=2nd | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=175 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/16108815 }}</ref> where the specific epithet combines the [[Latin]] ''ater'' meaning "black" with ''-ceps'' meaning "-capped" or "-headed".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=atriceps | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=atriceps | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=13 May 2026}}</ref> The imperial shag was formerly usually retained in the genus ''Phalacrocorax'', but has also sometimes been placed in its own genus ''Notocarbo''.<ref>{{Cite journal | last=Siegel-Causey | first=Douglas | date=1988 | title=Phylogeny of the Phalacrocoracidae | journal=The Condor | volume=90 | issue=4 | pages=885-905 | doi=10.2307/1368846 | url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/condor/vol90/iss4/15/ }}</ref> Based on a comprehensive molecular phylogeny of the cormorant family [[Phalacrocoracidae]] by Martyn Kennedy and Hamish Spencer that was published in 2014, the imperial shag is now one of nine species placed in the genus ''[[Leucocarbo]]'' that had been introduced by the French naturalist [[Charles Lucien Bonaparte]] in 1856.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Kennedy | first1=M. | last2=Spencer | first2=H.G. | date=2014 | title=Classification of the cormorants of the world | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=79 | pages=249-257 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2014.06.020}}</ref><ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=13 May 2026 }}</ref>

Eight [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=avilist/>
* ''L. a. atriceps'' ([[Phillip Parker King|King, PP]], 1828) – islands and coasts of south-central Chile and Argentina southward to [[Cape Horn]]
* ''L. a. albiventer'' ([[René Lesson|Lesson, RP]], 1831) – King shag – [[Falkland Islands]]{{efn|The validity of ''albiventer'' is questionable, and some authorities consider it only a black-cheeked [[Morph (biology)|morph]] of ''atriceps'' (''[[sensu stricto]]'').<ref name = Antarctic>{{cite book | last=Shirihai | first=H. | date=2002 | title=The Complete Guide to Antarctic Wildlife | publisher=Alula | isbn=0-691-11414-5 }}</ref><ref name = Chile>Jaramillo, A., Burker, P., & Beadle, D. (2003). ''Birds of Chile.'' Christopher Helm. {{ISBN|0-7136-4688-8}}</ref> This black-cheeked type occurs together with "normal" white-cheeked ''atriceps'' at some localities in southern mainland South America.<ref name = Chile/> There are no known behavioral isolating mechanism between the two and [[Hybrid (biology)|hybrids]] do occur.<ref>{{cite journal | last=Rasmussen | first=P.C. | author-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=1991 | title=Relationship between coastal South American King and Blue-eyed Shags | journal=Condor | volume=93 | issue=4 | pages=825–839 | doi=10.2307/3247717 | jstor=3247717 | url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/condor/vol93/iss4/5/ }}</ref><ref>{{ cite journal | last=Siegel-Causey | first=D. | date=1986 | title=The courtship behavior and mixed-species pairing of King and Imperial Blue-eyed Shags (''Phalacrocorax albiventer'' and ''P. atriceps'') | journal=Wilson Bulletin | volume=98 | issue=4 | pages=571-580 | url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/wilson_bulletin/vol98/iss4/9/ }}</ref>}}
* ''L. a. georgianus'' ([[Einar Lönnberg|Lönnberg, E]], 1906) – South Georgia shag – [[South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands]], and [[South Orkney Islands]]
* ''L. a. melanogenis'' ([[Edward Blyth|Blyth, E]], 1860) – Crozet shag – [[Prince Edward Islands|Prince Edward]], Marion, and [[Crozet Islands|Crozet]] islands (southern Indian Ocean, southeast of South Africa)
* ''L. a. bransfieldensis'' ([[Robert Cushman Murphy|Murphy, RC]], 1936) – Antarctic shag – [[South Shetland Islands]] and [[Antarctic Peninsula]]
* ''L. a. verrucosus'' ([[Jean Cabanis|Cabanis, JL]], 1875) – Kerguelen shag – [[Kerguelen Islands]] (southern Indian Ocean)
* ''L. a. nivalis'' ([[Robert Falla|Falla, RA]], 1937) – Heard Island shag – [[Heard Island]] (southern Indian Ocean)
* ''L. a. purpurascens'' ([[Johann Friedrich von Brandt|Brandt, JF]], 1837) – Macquarie shag – [[Macquarie Island]] and adjacent Bishop and Clerk Rocks (southeast of Australia)

[[File:Phalacrocorax atriceps2.jpg|thumb|right|A white-cheeked ''L. (a.) atriceps'' with black-cheeked ''L. (a.) albiventer'' on either side. [[Beagle Channel]], Argentina]]

While some authorities consider all of the above — except ''albiventer'' — as separate species,<ref name = HBW>{{ cite book | last=Orta | first=J. | year=1992 | chapter=Family Phalacrocoracidae (Cormorants) | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | volume=1: Ostrich to Ducks | location=Barcelona, Spain | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=84-87334-10-5 | pages=326–353 [350-351] | chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0001unse/page/350/mode/1up | chapter-url-access=registration }}</ref><ref name = Clements>Clements, J. F. (2007). ''[[The Clements Checklist of Birds of the World]].'' 6th edition. Christopher Helm. {{ISBN|978-0-7136-8695-1}}</ref> others consider all as subspecies of a single species<ref name=HANZAB>{{Citation|last1=Marchant |first1=S. |last2=Higgins |first2=P. J. |year=2002 |title=HANZAB species list |publisher=Birds Australia |url=http://www.birdsaustralia.com.au/hanzab/HANZAB_spp_list.pdf |access-date=2007-10-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070927161254/http://www.birdsaustralia.com.au/hanzab/HANZAB_spp_list.pdf |archive-date=2007-09-27 }}</ref> (as done in this article). Alternatively, some recognize two species, the white-cheeked ''L. atriceps'' (with subspecies ''bransfieldensis'', ''nivalis'' and ''georgianus'') and the black-cheeked ''L. albiventer'' (with subspecies ''melanogenis'' and ''purpurascens''),<ref name = Howard&Moore>[[Edward C. Dickinson|Dickinson, E. C.]] eds. (2003). ''[[Howard and Moore Complete Checklist of the Birds of the World]].'' 3d edition. Christopher Helm. {{ISBN|0-7136-6536-X}}</ref> or it has been suggested that three species should be recognized: ''L. atriceps'' (including ''albiventer''), ''L. georgianus'' (with subspecies ''bransfieldensis'' and ''nivalis''), and ''L. melanogenis'' (with subspecies ''purpurascens'' and possibly ''verrucosus'', though the latter is more distinctive, and most consider it a separate species, the [[Kerguelen shag]]).<ref name = Antarctic/>

All these taxa are now treated as subspecies of the imperial shag based on a molecular phylogenetic study by Nicolas Rawlence and collaborators that was published in 2022. The authors found that despite the wide separation between the breeding colonies on the subantarctic islands, the genetic differences are small suggesting a circumpolar dispersal from South America after the [[Last Glacial Maximum]] around 21 thousand years ago.<ref name=avilist/><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Rawlence | first1=N.J. | last2=Salis | first2=A.T. | last3=Spencer | first3=H.G. | last4=Waters | first4=J.M. | last5=Scarsbrook | first5=L. | last6=Mitchell | first6=K.J. | last7=Phillips | first7=R.A. | last8=Calderón | first8=L. | last9=Cook | first9=T.R. | last10=Bost | first10=C.-A. | last11=Dutoit | first11=L. | last12=King | first12=T.M. | last13=Masello | first13=J.F. | last14=Nupen | first14=L.J. | last15=Quillfeldt | first15=P. | last16=Ratcliffe | first16=N. | last17=Ryan | first17=P.G. | last18=Till | first18=C.E. | last19=Kennedy | first19=M. | date=2022 | title=Rapid radiation of Southern Ocean shags in response to receding sea ice | journal=Journal of Biogeography | volume=49 | issue=5 | pages=942-953 | doi=10.1111/jbi.14360 | doi-access=free}}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Cormoran Shag.jpg|thumb|left|An immature ''L. (a.) albiventer'' in [[Patagonia]], Argentina]]
The imperial shag has a total length of {{convert|70|-|79|cm|abbr=on}} and weighs {{convert|1.8|-|3.5|kg|abbr=on}}, with males usually larger than females.<ref name = Antarctic/> It is endowed with glossy black feathers covering most of its body, with a white belly and neck. It possesses a distinctive ring of blue skin around its eyes, an orange-yellow nasal knob, pinkish legs and feet, and an erectile black crest.<ref name = HBW/> During the non-breeding season, adults lack the crest, have a duller facial area, and less or no white to the back and wings.<ref name = Antarctic/> It has a serrated bill used for catching fish.

The group varies primarily in the amount of white on the cheeks/ear-coverts, [[wing-coverts]] and back. Most [[taxa]] have white cheeks and ear-coverts, but these are black in ''albiventer'', ''purpurascens'' and ''melanogenis''.<ref name = Antarctic/> Chicks are uniform brownish, and immatures are brownish and white (instead of black and white), have dull facial skin, and lack the orange-yellow nasal knob and blue eye-ring.<ref name = Antarctic/>

==Behavior==

===Breeding===
[[File:Imperial Shags.jpg|thumb|300px|right|A large colony of ''L. (a.) albiventer'' at the Beagle Channel, Argentina. Notice the numerous all-brownish chicks.]]
This is a [[Seabird colony|colonial]], [[monogamy in animals|monogamous]] species. The colonies are usually relatively small, but some consist of hundreds of pairs and are often shared with other seabirds such as [[rock shag]]s, [[southern rockhopper penguin]]s and [[black-browed albatross]]es.<ref name = Antarctic/><ref name =HBW/> Up to five eggs (usually two or three) are placed in a nest made of seaweed and grass, and cemented together with mud and excrement.<ref name =HBW/> The eggs usually hatch in about five weeks, and are brooded by both parents.<ref name="encyclopedia">{{Citation |title=Antarctica: an encyclopedia from Abbot Ice Shelf to zooplankton|last=Mary |first=Trewby |year=2002 |publisher=Firefly Books Ltd. |location=Auckland, New Zealand |isbn=1-55297-590-8 |page=38}}</ref> Many chicks and eggs are lost to predators such as [[skua]]s and [[sheathbill]]s.<ref name="encyclopedia" />

===Feeding===
The diet of this species consists of small [[benthic]] fish, [[crustacean]]s, [[polychaete]]s, [[gastropods]] and [[octopuses]].<ref name = HBW/> They primarily feed on fish, especially [[Argentine anchoita]],.<ref name = SouthAmerica>Punta, G., Yorio P., and Herrera, G. (2003). ''[http://cat.inist.fr/?aModele=afficheN&cpsidt=15675130 Temporal patterns in the diet and food partitioning in imperial cormorants (Phalacrocorax atriceps) and rock shags (P. magellanicus) breeding at Bahía Bustamante, Argentina.]'' Wilson Bulletin 115(3): 307-315</ref> Mean diving depth is almost {{convert|25|m|abbr=on}}, and they have been filmed diving as deep as {{convert|60|m|abbr=on}} to forage on the sea floor.<ref name = SouthAmerica/><ref name="Selfies">{{cite journal |last1=Gómez-Laich |first1=Agustina |last2=Yoda |first2=Ken |last3=Zavalaga |first3=Carlos |last4=Quintana |first4=Flavio |title=Selfies of Imperial Cormorants (Phalacrocorax atriceps): What Is Happening Underwater? |journal=PLOS ONE |date=14 September 2015 |volume=10 |issue=9 |article-number=e0136980 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0136980 |pmid=26367384 |issn=1932-6203|pmc=4569182 |bibcode=2015PLoSO..1036980G |doi-access=free }}</ref> Most feeding takes place in inshore regions, but at least some populations will travel some distance from the shore to fish.<ref name = Antarctic/>

==Conservation status==
Overall this species is not considered [[threatened]] and is consequently listed as [[Least Concern]] by [[BirdLife International]] and [[IUCN]].<ref name=IUCN/> Most subspecies are relatively common, with estimates of over 10,000 pairs of each.<ref name=HeardIsland>Department of the Environment, Water, Heritage and the Arts (2009). ''[http://www.heardisland.aq/nature/animals/fact_file_heard_island_cormorant.html Heard Island Cormorant.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090912085459/http://www.heardisland.aq/nature/animals/fact_file_heard_island_cormorant.html |date=2009-09-12 }}'' Heard Island & McDonald Island. Accessed 2009-01-21.</ref>
